ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice noodle roll entry; no specific you tiao variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice noodle rolls — composite dish with fried dough filling
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific you tiao chee cheong fun code. A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Singaporean composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of you tiao chee cheong fun in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising two steamed rice noodle rolls each wrapped around a piece of you tiao, dressed with sweet soy sauce and sesame seeds.

nutrient_source.title: You Tiao Chee Cheong Fun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for You Tiao Chee Cheong Fun (cr_id F05100153). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice noodle roll and fried dough composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for you tiao chee cheong fun as a composite dish. GI of 68 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain rice noodles / chee cheong fun (GI ~61–70, medium band) combined with the known high-GI contribution of deep-fried wheat dough (you tiao, GI ~74–76). A blended weighted estimate of 68 is used, consistent with the medium GI band. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 50.88) / 100 ≈ 34.6 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 541 mg per 100 g — elevated sodium from sweet soy sauce, hoisin sauce, and you tiao components
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid you tiao chee cheong fun due to its sodium content of 541 mg per 100 g; if consumed, request reduced sauce and restrict portion to no more than 100 g to keep sodium intake within a single meal's safe threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-high GI of 68 combined with 25.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and low dietary fibre of 0.89 g per 100 g, producing a high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume you tiao chee cheong fun sparingly; the combination of refined rice flour noodles, deep-fried wheat dough, and sweet soy sauce creates a high glycemic load (GL ≈ 34.6 per standard serving), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation — reduce sweet sauce and limit portion to 100 g or less.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11 g per 100 g including saturated fat ~1.8 g and trace trans fat ~0.1 g from deep-fried you tiao, combined with high sodium 541 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit you tiao chee cheong fun due to its elevated sodium (541 mg/100 g), total fat from deep frying, and trace trans fat in the you tiao component, all of which contribute to endothelial stress and blood pressure elevation; occasional consumption with reduced sauce and a small portion is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried you tiao generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium 541 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; trace trans fat is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid you tiao chee cheong fun; the deep-fried you tiao component is a hard AGE disqualifier that worsens joint inflammation, and the high sodium and trace trans fat further exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling and systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: You Tiao Chee Cheong Fun (油条猪肠粉) is a popular Singaporean Chinese breakfast and snack dish consisting of smooth, silky steamed rice noodle rolls (chee cheong fun) wrapped around crispy Chinese fried dough fritters (you tiao, also known as you char kway or Chinese crullers). The dish is served with a drizzle of sweet soy sauce and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. The rice noodle rolls are made from a thin batter of rice flour and water, steamed on flat trays to produce translucent, tender sheets that are then rolled around the you tiao. The you tiao is a deep-fried leavened wheat dough stick with a light, airy interior and a golden, slightly crispy exterior. The textural contrast between the soft, slippery rice noodle and the crunchy fried dough is a defining characteristic of this dish. Nutritionally, the dish provides 214 kcal per 100 g, with 25.4 g carbohydrate, 11 g fat, and 3.4 g protein. Sodium is elevated at 541 mg per 100 g, primarily from the sweet soy sauce and hoisin sauce accompaniments. Dietary fibre is low at 0.89 g per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 68 (medium band), and a standard 200 g serving carries a high glycemic load of approximately 34.6, making this dish a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The deep-fried you tiao component also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is widely available at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and dim sum establishments across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100153) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest defensible rice noodle and fried dough composite analogues, documented transparently. Multilingual names are well-established for this canonical Singaporean dish.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g of 1.8 g is estimated from the HPB database context for this dish type; the HPB entry does not separately report saturated fat — value is consistent with the fat profile of deep-fried wheat dough and rice noodle composite dishes per USDA FoodData Central analogues. 2. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g is a trace estimate consistent with deep-fried you tiao prepared with commercial vegetable shortening; this is documented in the health_suitability input and retained as a trace value. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this specific Singaporean composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for you tiao chee cheong fun as a composite dish has been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. is_vegan set to false because sweet soy sauce formulations in Singapore commonly contain non-vegan additives; is_halal set to false because you tiao is traditionally prepared with lard in some establishments — halal certification varies by vendor. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 200 g standard serving: (68 × 50.88) / 100 = 34.6.
